elements.html:
Dr Zachary Howard
School of Psychological Sciences
The University of Western Australia
35 Stirling Highway, Crawley WA 6009
Email: Zach.Howard@uwa.edu.au

SONA Debrief Form

Project title: Understanding the factors contributing to working memory demands

Supervisor: Dr Zachary Howard (CI)

Student Researcher:Ms Nirvana Singh (Honours)

1. What was I asked to do, and why was I asked to do it?

Today, you completed a working memory task called the N-back task. In this task, you had to decide whether the image on the screen was the same as the previous one (easy version) or the same as three images ago (hard version). You did four rounds in total: two rounds of the easy version and two rounds of the hard version. In one round of each version, you also had a prospective memory task to do. This consisted of you responding differently if the image showed clothing or a food item, based on the instructions you were given.

We are interested in changes in your N-back task response time and accuracy depending on prospective memory task presence. We are also interested in how your performance changed depending on whether you were doing the easy or hard version of the N-back task. Analysing your results will help us to figure out how prospective memory cost works.

2. What is the psychological phenomenon this study tries to understand or explain?

The experiment aims to further understand prospective memory cost. Prospective memory is the kind of memory used when humans remember to perform actions in the future while they are busily engaged in other tasks. Prospective memory cost describes the cost in task performance that occurs when someone is completing an ongoing task (like the N-back task) and a prospective memory task at the same time. Typically we see that people are slower and/or less accurate in an ongoing task (like an N-Back, or lexical decision task) when they have a prospective memory task to do. But we don’t understand exactly how this cost occurs, i.e., what cognitive process causes the PM cost?

3. Why is it important this research is done?

By understanding what causes prospective memory cost, we may be able to develop strategies to reduce it so people’s performance doesn’t have to suffer in this context. Reducing prospective memory costs is vital for people working in medicine, aviation, or other high-risk environments where specific actions must be completed in correct orders, in response to specific events, or at specific times. But in all walks of life people have to remember to do things (we might need to remember to pick our child up at a certain time, take our pills each morning, or buy bread on our way home from work).

4. What is a proposed explanation for the psychological phenomenon that is being tested in this study? That is, what is the hypothesis?

There are two explanations for prospective memory cost. One is that the cost occurs because cognitive capacity is shared between the ongoing task and the prospective memory task. This is called the capacity-sharing theory. The other explanation hypothesises that the cost is due to increased caution. This is called the delay theory. This study will use computational modelling to figure out whether the data collected supports the capacity-sharing theory or delay theory.

5. What are some critical design consideration that needed to be built into the study to enable testing this hypothesis?

We needed both an easy and a hard N-back condition in order to test a specific hypothesis that PM costs only occur under high demand. Developing a paradigm that induced working memory demand, while also facilitating the inclusion of a PM task that was sufficiently demanding to induce a cost, required careful design of the N-Back task with reference to prior literature. To minimize fatigue and practice effects, the study was pseudo-randomised, ensuring that participants did not complete the prospective memory task in consecutive rounds.

6. How can I learn more about this topic?

Strickland, L., Heathcote, A., Remington, R. W., & Loft, S. (2017). Accumulating Evidence About What Prospective Memory Costs Actually Reveal. Journal of Experimental Psychology. Learning, Memory, and Cognition, 43(10), 1616–1629. https://doi.org/10.1037/xlm0000400

Einstein, G. O., & McDaniel, M. A. (1990). Normal aging and prospective memory. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, memory, and cognition, 16(4), 717.

7. What was the researcher’s pathway to get where they are today?

The researcher, Nirvana Singh, received her Bachelor of Psychology in 2023, and is now completing her Honours dissertation in 2025.

The supervisor, Dr Zach Howard, received his PhD from the University of Newcastle in 2020, and completed a post-doc at UWA for three years. He is currently an Australian Research Council Fellow at UWA and holds an appointment with Defence Science and Technology Group as well.
